meta.title: Projects | {{name}}
meta.description: Migration projects Handy Terminal System Migration, Legacy JSP to C# WMS Migration, and the personal project LearnOva Online Learning Platform.

title: Projects
description: From Handy device and legacy WMS migration to a full-stack learning platform — each project tells a Problem → Analysis → Solution → Result story.
empty: No projects match the selected filter.

handy.shortDescription: Migration of the Handy system from BHT-OS/BHT-BASIC to Android + VB.NET PC communication while keeping the existing server and database.
handy.overview: Handy Terminal System Migration moves the handheld application from the proprietary BHT platform to Android on Keyence BT-A1000, while updating the PC Communication app in VB.NET. Existing server and database are preserved to avoid changing operational business behavior.
handy.context: The legacy system ran on dedicated BHT devices with BHT-OS and BHT-BASIC. The Handy app communicated with a PC Communication app, then connected to production server and database.

problems.p1: Proprietary BHT platform, hard to extend and integrate with modern technology.
problems.p2: BHT-BASIC source is difficult to maintain and tied to aging hardware.
problems.p3: Hardware replacement is hard when BHT devices are no longer supported.
problems.p4: Handy ↔ PC ↔ Server communication flow must remain intact after migration.
problems.p5: Risk of business mismatch if code is moved without behavioral comparison.

goals.g1: Move the Handy app to Android on Keyence BT-A1000 devices.
goals.g2: Update the PC Communication application in VB.NET.
goals.g3: Keep the existing server and database unchanged.
goals.g4: Preserve business flows and operational procedures.
goals.g5: Ensure stable data transmission across all tiers after migration.

handy.role: Software Developer Intern — legacy source analysis, feature conversion support, and data flow verification.
handy.scope: Android Handy App ↔ VB.NET Communication App ↔ Existing Server / Database
handy.techDescription: Android, VB.NET, BHT-BASIC, Keyence BT-A1000, Existing Server, Existing Database.
handy.architectureDescription: Android Handy communicates with the VB.NET PC app; the PC app connects to the existing server/database. Server tier is preserved to reduce migration risk.

responsibilities.r1: Studied BHT-BASIC source and processing flows on legacy devices.
responsibilities.r2: Analyzed data send/receive flows between Handy, PC, and server.
responsibilities.r3: Cross-checked legacy system behavior against business requirements.
responsibilities.r4: Supported conversion of features to the Android application.
responsibilities.r5: Adjusted PC-side communication logic in VB.NET.
responsibilities.r6: Verified end-to-end data flows after each migrated feature.
responsibilities.r7: Ensured business behavior matched the pre-migration system.
responsibilities.r8: Supported testing on Keyence BT-A1000 hardware.

highlights.h1: More than a language change — device and platform shift while preserving operations.
highlights.h2: Legacy server and database reused, narrowing infrastructure change scope.
highlights.h3: Behavioral comparison workflow before finalizing Android/VB.NET logic.

challenges.c1: BHT-BASIC source is hard to read with limited standardized documentation.
challenges.c2: Aligning new Android and VB.NET PC app behavior with legacy protocol.
challenges.c3: Testing depends on real Keyence devices and production-like environments.

solutions.s1: Traced each legacy screen/feature and recorded inputs and outputs.
solutions.s2: Migrated in small business-flow slices with step-by-step verification.
solutions.s3: Coordinated BT-A1000 testing and compared data logs with the legacy system.

results.res1: Handy features migrated to Android with behavior matching the legacy system.
results.res2: VB.NET PC communication app stable with new devices.
results.res3: Server/database unchanged, reducing deployment downtime and risk.

learnings.l1: Device migration requires understanding protocol and business logic, not just new UI.
learnings.l2: Keeping the server tier narrows test scope when clients change.
learnings.l3: Behavior comparison notes are valuable assets for migration teams.

wms.shortDescription: Migration of WMS features from JSP to C#/.NET with business, data, and Excel report verification so the new system matches the old one.
wms.overview: Legacy JSP to C# WMS Migration moves warehouse management features from Java/JSP to C#/.NET with Application/Infrastructure separation. Focus is on preserving business logic, PostgreSQL data, and Excel report output accuracy.
wms.context: The legacy system used Java/JSP with business logic embedded in legacy source, Excel templates for reports, and PostgreSQL. The new system uses C#/.NET with layered architecture and the same PostgreSQL database.

problems.p1: Business logic scattered in JSP is hard to trace and reuse.
problems.p2: Risk of mismatch when converting Java syntax to C# without understanding business rules.
problems.p3: Excel reports depend on templates and complex monthly aggregation.
problems.p4: Permissions, error popups, and edge cases are missed with happy-path-only testing.
problems.p5: Continuous comparison between old and new outputs is required.

goals.g1: Migrate JSP features to C#/.NET within WMS scope.
goals.g2: Preserve business flows, permissions, and error handling.
goals.g3: Map related PostgreSQL tables and queries correctly.
goals.g4: Ensure Excel (.xlsx) reports match legacy output.
goals.g5: Update business documentation and logic difference notes.

wms.role: Software Developer Intern — JSP analysis, C# conversion support, data and Excel report verification.
wms.scope: Legacy JSP screens → C# Application/Infrastructure → PostgreSQL → Excel report output
wms.techDescription: Java, JSP, C#, .NET, PostgreSQL, SQL, Excel template, Git.
wms.architectureDescription: Flow from legacy JSP through logic analysis, database mapping, C#/.NET implementation, comparison testing, and Excel export. PostgreSQL is the shared data source between both systems.

responsibilities.r1: Read and analyzed legacy JSP source to identify business flows.
responsibilities.r2: Identified related PostgreSQL tables and queries per feature.
responsibilities.r3: Compared logic between old and new systems.
responsibilities.r4: Supported conversion of features from JSP to C#.
responsibilities.r5: Verified permission conditions, error messages, and popups.
responsibilities.r6: Checked queries and output data after migration.
responsibilities.r7: Supported Excel report creation/verification — e.g. 帳票_個人日報一覧 / Personal Daily Report List.
responsibilities.r8: Wrote or updated business documentation and logic difference cases.

highlights.h1: Business-focused migration — not just Java → C# syntax conversion.
highlights.h2: Excel report verification for 帳票_個人日報一覧 / Personal Daily Report List by employee and month.
highlights.h3: Systematic old/new output comparison for queries and aggregated data.

challenges.c1: JSP logic mixed into views makes business extraction time-consuming.
challenges.c2: Multi-worksheet Excel reports with monthly totals are prone to numeric drift.
challenges.c3: Permission and session edge cases are hard to reproduce without test data.

solutions.s1: Mapped input/output flows and DB tables before writing C#.
solutions.s2: Compared old/new Excel files worksheet by worksheet and monthly totals.
solutions.s3: Recorded every logic difference for review with mentor/BA.

results.res1: Migrated WMS features behave consistently with legacy business logic.
results.res2: Excel reports export with correct structure and expected figures.
results.res3: Updated business documentation supports post-migration maintenance.

learnings.l1: WMS migration requires understanding reports and aggregation, not just CRUD.
learnings.l2: Comparison testing is mandatory when replacing the backend stack.
learnings.l3: Logic difference notes reduce regression risk after go-live.

learnova.shortDescription: Personal online learning platform with HLS streaming, CloudFront, PayOS webhooks, and revenue/commission logic — built as a portfolio demo.
learnova.overview: LearnOva Online Learning Platform is a full-stack personal project: course management, HLS video playback, PayOS payments, and revenue logic between platform and instructors. Features are built for a real portfolio demo; some areas can still be extended.
learnova.context: A personal project to practice React + Spring Boot + PostgreSQL + AWS (S3, MediaConvert, CloudFront) and PayOS integration with webhook-based payment status updates.

problems.p1: Large source videos require an HLS conversion pipeline and CDN delivery.
problems.p2: Online payments need webhook handling and UI failure after successful payment.
problems.p3: Revenue and instructor commission logic must be clear and verifiable.
problems.p4: Full-stack demo must balance feature scope and development time.
problems.p5: Multilingual support and learning content need consistent data structure.

goals.g1: Build registration/login and course management flows.
goals.g2: Deliver HLS video via CloudFront after MediaConvert processing.
goals.g3: Integrate PayOS and webhooks for payment status updates.
goals.g4: Manage revenue and instructor commission calculation.
goals.g5: Deploy a portfolio demo with Docker and clear documentation.

learnova.role: Full-stack Developer — API design, React frontend, video pipeline, and payment integration.
learnova.scope: React Frontend → Spring Boot API → PostgreSQL → S3/MediaConvert/CloudFront → PayOS Webhook
learnova.techDescription: React, Vite, Java, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, HLS.js, AWS S3, CloudFront, MediaConvert, PayOS, Docker.
learnova.architectureDescription: React frontend calls Spring Boot API; videos upload to S3, convert to HLS via MediaConvert, and stream through CloudFront. PayOS webhooks update orders and trigger revenue logic.

responsibilities.r1: Designed PostgreSQL schema for users, courses, payments, and revenue.
responsibilities.r2: Built Spring Boot REST APIs for auth, courses, and payments.
responsibilities.r3: Integrated HLS.js on React and upload/convert video pipeline.
responsibilities.r4: Configured S3, MediaConvert, and CloudFront for streaming.
responsibilities.r5: Integrated PayOS and handled webhook status updates.
responsibilities.r6: Implemented revenue and instructor commission logic.
responsibilities.r7: Handled edge case: payment success while UI encounters an error.
responsibilities.r8: Dockerized and prepared the portfolio demo.

highlights.h1: HLS video + CloudFront — production-like streaming practice on AWS.
highlights.h2: PayOS webhooks with reliable payment status update flow.
highlights.h3: Revenue/commission logic reflecting a real marketplace problem.

challenges.c1: MediaConvert pipeline and CDN require correct permissions and URLs.
challenges.c2: Payment webhooks must be idempotent and handle retries.
challenges.c3: UI state sync when payment callback is slow or the tab closes early.

solutions.s1: Separated upload, convert, and HLS manifest publish steps clearly.
solutions.s2: Stored payment intent and confirmed access via webhook before unlocking courses.
solutions.s3: Polling/refresh order status when users return after payment.

results.res1: Portfolio demo with end-to-end course purchase and HLS playback.
results.res2: PayOS webhooks update orders and revenue to the correct state.
results.res3: Full-stack architecture ready for additional instructor features.

learnings.l1: Streaming is not frontend-only — storage, transcode, and CDN matter.
learnings.l2: Payment integration must be designed for failure and duplicate webhooks.
learnings.l3: Personal projects work best when the problem is close to real production.
